original_abstract: Abstract In this chapter, the historical development of the relationship between the central and local governments in Japan will be analyzed in terms of three aspects – administration, finance, and politics – and the characteristics of the relationship between the central and local governments in Japan will be clarified by setting a framework of interfusion and separation. When modernizing, Japan studied the local government systems in place throughout the European continent and chose to adopt the interfusion model of the relationship between central and local governments, whereby the central government delegates its policies to local governments to ensure their implementation. The relationship between the central and local governments in Japan has undergone changes through functional centralization during the wartime regime, decentralization through reforms implemented during the Occupation, and decentralization reforms that have been taking place since the 1990s. However, the characteristics of interfusion have been maintained and reinforced through municipal mergers and the development of a system of fiscal adjustments. While Japan has a high ratio of local government to total government activities relative to other countries, this characteristic of Japan is supported by an interfusion-type relationship between the central and local governments.
humanized_abstract: 1. Historical Development of Relationships between Administration, Finance and Politics in Local Public Organizations and Identification of Characteristics of the Central—Local Government Relationship in Japan This chapter will examine the development of the central—local government relationship in Japan in the context of the three aspects of administration, finance and politics. On the basis of an interfusion—separation framework, it clarifies the characteristics of the central—local government relationship in Japan and probes into the underlying causes. In the process of modernizing the local public organizations in Japan, the model of the local government system in the European Continent was referred to as a reference. The model of central—local government relationship adopted in Japan was interfusion, in which the central government delegates its policies to local governments and requires them to carry them out. The relationships between the central and local governments in Japan have gone through the stages of functional centralization during wartime period, the decentralization during the Occupation regime and the decentralization reform since the 1990s. In the meantime, the interfusion features have been retained and even enhanced by the process of municipal mergers and the implementation of fiscal readjustments. It is shown that Japan has one of the largest proportions of local government activities to total public activities when compared with other developed countries. As discussed in this chapter, this characteristic of Japan is within the framework of the interfusion type of the central—local government relationship.
